titleStmt.title: Letter from Celia Duffin to her mother, Maria Duffin, 10 February 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Celia Duffin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter is part of a rich correspondence between various members of the Duffin family, a large prosperous family living in Strandtown, Belfast. Several family members served in the war, including Major Terence Duffin, who served as a staff officer with 107 Brigade, and later with Royal Irish Rifles; Major Charles G Duffin MC, Royal Field Artillery; and their sisters Emma, Celia and Dorothy who served as Voluntary Aid Detachments (VADS) in Egypt and France, and with the YMCA.
Celia Duffin was a VAD with the 3rd London General Hospital in Wandsworth which opened in 1914. She was an accomplished poet, publishing, amongst other volumes, a book of poetry with her sister Ruth.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
25 Nicoria Rd Feb 10th 1916 Dearest Mamma I find it is a week since I left
home and I have never written yet, but
I have spent my off duty time there
last 3 days hunting for Olive. I
have never had a more peaceful journey
no the least bit sick either on the
boat or the train, and fast asleep
all night. I had great greetings
with my friend the Captain and paraded
the deck with him until everybody
else had gone to bed, and he told
me all his adventures. I always also
made friends with a young subaltern
going to join the Irish Fusiliers at Kent,
and a rather nice lady who
took an interest in me because her two
daughters were V.A.D's at Rowen. But
there was nobody interesting to talk to
in the train. I lunched at Victoria
before coming on here and found Gosse
had an afternoon off, so we lit a
nice fire and I made some good tea
and fed her on currant loaf which
she thought awfully good. It was awfully nice seeing you all again,
I felt as if I had been away for ages.
I got a message from Olive saying that
she and Marjorie had arrived at the
Strand Palace Hotel, and as I happened
to be off duty at 5, I hurried up
to train without waiting for tea. When I
arrived I could find no trace of them,
and after waiting some time
feeling very hungry, I had some tea
and returned home. The hotel was full of new army men and their relations
and best girls. Next morning I got a
card saying they were at the Regent
Palace Hotel and as I had 3 hours
off in the morning, I went in again,
and again couldn't find them, although
Olive says they were there all the time.
However, next day, I was off in the after-
noon, so <wired to Olive to meet me
at S. Rensington Station which is half
way here, and actually did - Marjorie
however had gone. We went to the muse-
um which is quite close and saw
such fascinating things there, especially
in the old furniture part, which is all
arranged in such charming old-fashioned
rooms, The old chaises are attractive
too, and we had a glimpse at the
minitures. Poor Olive had a bad cold
again.
I am awfully glad about your £500 but
wish Jon had got the portrait, why did
Miss Munn make that up? Surely there
must have been more money than Jon
thought? How nice of her to leave something
to us. I have just heard too that our
allowances are to be cut down. The War
Office is waking up. I am just trying some of the soup for
supper, it is simmering at present and
smells good, I added a little milk
and butter and will keep some
hot when Gosse comes in late. No news of any kind, I am back in
the same ward, so far, all is peace
with me, but the two staff nurses seem
to have had a row this morning and
the head sister is still away. Many thanks for your and Helen's
letter. Ever your loving Celia
